prompt:
Scheduler-core ticket, dispatch queue rewrite. Context: our dispatcher pops the lowest-cost job next, but ops tooling constantly touches SPECIFIC in-flight entries — expedite this one, cancel that one — and the current BinaryHeap forces a full rebuild for every touch. Worse, we've promised customers a fairness SLA: two jobs at the same cost dispatch in the order they were enqueued, no exceptions, and BinaryHeap gives us arbitrary tie order.

Build the replacement: crate rs-handleheap (lib name rs_handleheap, Cargo.toml is already there — don't touch it or .gitignore). Stdlib only. tests/handleheap.rs is the acceptance contract, byte for byte: `cargo test --offline` green with that file untouched.

Public API:

- `pub struct Handle` — opaque ticket for one enqueued entry, `#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Hash)]`. Handles are NEVER reused, ever — a fresh push must not mint a handle equal to any past one, even after pops/removes.
- `#[derive(Debug, PartialEq, Eq)] pub enum HeapError { StaleHandle, WouldIncrease { current: u64, requested: u64 } }`
- `pub struct HandleHeap<T>` with:
  - `new()` and `Default`, `len() -> usize`, `is_empty() -> bool`
  - `push(&mut self, priority: u64, value: T) -> Handle`
  - `peek(&self) -> Option<(Handle, u64, &T)>` — no consumption
  - `pop(&mut self) -> Option<(Handle, u64, T)>` — the returned handle is already stale (we log it)
  - `decrease_key(&mut self, h: Handle, new_priority: u64) -> Result<(), HeapError>`
  - `remove(&mut self, h: Handle) -> Result<(u64, T), HeapError>` — cancel any live entry, not just the head
  - `contains(&self, h: Handle) -> bool`, `priority_of(&self, h: Handle) -> Option<u64>`

Semantics, all of which the tests pin exactly:

- Min-queue: lowest priority pops first. Ties break by enqueue order — think of every push as taking a monotonically increasing sequence number; ordering is (priority, sequence). That rank is assigned at push and NEVER changes: an entry that gets decrease_key'd into an existing priority class sits according to its ORIGINAL enqueue rank, not at the back, not at the front.
- decrease_key means decrease. Equal to current: Ok, no-op (rank untouched). Greater than current: Err(WouldIncrease { current, requested }) and the heap must be byte-for-byte undisturbed — delaying a job goes through cancel+re-enqueue in a different audit path, never through this method.
- Stale handles (already popped, already removed, or never this heap's) get Err(StaleHandle) from decrease_key/remove, false from contains, None from priority_of. Staleness wins over WouldIncrease when both would apply.
- remove must work from the MIDDLE of the heap and leave dispatch order perfect afterward. Fair warning from the last time someone wrote this: when you fill the hole left by a removal with the last element, that element may need to move TOWARD the root, not only away from it. There's a test shaped to catch exactly that.
- Keep a handle→slot index in sync through every sift so decrease_key/remove are O(log n) — a linear scan per op is what we're escaping. (We won't measure complexity in tests, but a 400-op scripted workload cross-checks every observable against a reference model, so behavior has nowhere to hide.)

T gets no trait bounds. No panics on any path exercised by the contract; misuse is signalled through HeapError.
